display_title: 1986-01_02 — Bearing 315 - Newsletter of Cascade Orienteering Club - Jan/Feb 1986
body_html:
Page 1

Bearing 315 - Newsletter of Cascade Orienteering Club - Jan/Feb 1986

Season Greetings from Mari Gabrielsen of Norway to Cascade Orienteers,

I would like to say thank you to all of you I met during the two weekends I ran orienteering in the Seattle area. {Editor's note: Mari ran at the Cle Elum Ridge, Durr Road, and Newcastle Island meets during her two week stay.} You were so nice to me, and I really enjoyed orienteering in Washington (and Canada). It's fun to see that orienteers are nice people all over the world!

I hope some of you will come to Norway and try orienteering in my country. I would be glad to hear from you if and when you are coming, or if it's something I can do for you. I also hope I can come back to the U.S. for some other events.

Good luck with the 1986 O-season and with the work to do to make orienteering known all over the U.S.

Mari Gabrielsen
2090 Hurdal
NORWAY

P.S. Yes, this is my complete address!

Also on the International O-wire ...

Peter Haynes who has been active in running (2:40 Seattle Marathon) and orienteering in the northwest for the past two years (when he wasn't busy delivering academic papers around the world related to his work in atmospheric science) has returned to England, his home and place of future employ. Peter can be reached via post at Queen's College, Cambridge CB3 9ET, ENGLAND.

O-Club Publicity Campaign Moves into the Big Time on KOMO TV

Cascade's creative Publicity Chairperson, Roger Sharp, arranged for and coordinated a public service annoucement to be aired on KOMO TV's "Northwest Afternoon" program on Monday, December 9th. In exchange for bringing 15 people to participate in the audience of their show, KOMO TV agrees to run a 10-second public service announcement to help promote the cause of the nonprofit organization of your choice, in this case, the Cascade Orienteering Club. Supporting Roger and the Cascade Orienteering Club in the audience were Betty Berreth, Linda Dixon, Linda Fife, Joe Hudson, Robert Miller, Debbie Newell, Erin Renshaw, Ginny Rien, Linda Sharp, Elizabeth Smith, Francis Smith, Jan Vrana, Ken Wiljanen, and Eileen Williams. Roger and Robert spent about four hours distilling the essence of orienteering down to ten seconds. The final version, which Robert was elected to deliver, went as follows: "Have fun while staying in shape with the new sport that's like a treasure hunt - orienteering! This Saturday in Woodland Park at 10 a.m. Follow the orange and white signs." Did it work? Well, here's a quote from a Christmas card Robert received from an Orcas Island friend this year. "Guess what! I saw you on Northwest Afternoon including your commercial for orienteering. I was so excited ..." Many thanks to Steve Smalley, Cindy Rinehart, Dick Foley, Dana Middleton and the rest of the staff and crew at KOMO TV for airing our O-spot.

Page 2

O-meet Results

1985 Western Canadian Orienteering Championships, August 31 & September 1

This year's meet was held in southeastern British Columbia about 15 miles north of Cranbrook. The terrain was mostly open and very runable. The foothills of the Canadian Rockies provided a magnificent backdrop for the competition area. Our Canadian hosts put on a superbly run event complete with clear weather, an outdoor barbecue, and dancing at the local tavern. The 138 competitors included the following Stateside orienteers:

Discovery Park Night-O - Sunday, November 23, 1985

A small but hardy group of orienteers turned out for the night meet at Discovery Park. Several inches of snow and temperatures in the low teens kept more reasonable people inside. The rest (less reasonable) of us enjoyed courses set by Mike Schuh and pizza and pumpkin pie at the potluck hosted by Mike after the meet. Thanks to Mike Schuh for setting courses, to Debbie Newell for registration, to Arnold Kay for timing, and to the Miller's for use of their lantern and blanket ... burrrrr!

Crystal Springs Ski-O, Sunday, December 22, 1985

The sunshine at the Snoqualmie Pass Ski-O was a pleasant relief from fog-bound Seattle. Thanks to Mike Schuh for single-handedly setting courses, registering, timing and tending the fire with wood supplied by his father, Tom. Thanks also to the Sons of Norway Trollhaugen Lodge caretaker, Don Ahlf, for helping groom the trails and provide logistical support for Mike. And thanks to all for helping to pickup controls, especially Don Denbo, Rick Breseman and Eileen Williams who designed their own freestyle course.

Short Course - 4 km, 6 controls
* Sandy Miller - 82:23

Long Course - 6 km, 8 controls
Debbie Newell - 72:35 Robert Miller - 76:40
* Ken Wiljanen - 82:51

Page 3

Woodland Park, Saturday, December 14, 1985

Course Designer and Setter - Harvey Friedman
Assistant Meet Director - Arnold Kay
Greeter and Registrar - Roger Sharp
School League Registrar - Mike Schuh
Timer - Eileen Williams
Sign Placer and Plucker - Sandy Miller
Control Pluckers - Debbie Newell, et al.
Post-meet Publicity - Dick Diesner

About 60 people attending with a dozen of those helping, made the Woodland park meet a success. We received three of the special $1.00 Introduction to Orienteering Coupons which Roger designed - so they do work. Since the coupons expire February 8th, be sure to distribute any you have before the next local Cascade meet is held at Carkeek Park on January 18th. Many thanks to Jim Siscel and Boy Scout Troop 304, and Tom Hutchinson and Boy Scout Troop 16 for their enthusiastic participation and help with timing. Welcome to new and renewed members Marcia Stein, Tony Halstead, Troop 16 and Troop 304. And thanks to all for responding to the O-meet survey. Your comments and compliments are helpful for giving the club's board members ideas for making meets run more smoothly.

block_index.text: Bearing 315 - Newsletter of Cascade Orienteering Club - Jan/Feb 1986
Season Greetings from Mari Gabrielsen of Norway to Cascade Orienteers,

I would like to say thank you to all of you I met during the two weekends I ran orienteering in the Seattle area. {Editor's note: Mari ran at the Cle Elum Ridge, Durr Road, and Newcastle Island meets during her two week stay.} You were so nice to me, and I really enjoyed orienteering in Washington (and Canada). It's fun to see that orienteers are nice people all over the world!

I hope some of you will come to Norway and try orienteering in my country. I would be glad to hear from you if and when you are coming, or if it's something I can do for you. I also hope I can come back to the U.S. for some other events.
Good luck with the 1986 O-season and with the work to do to make orienteering known all over the U.S.
P.S. Yes, this is my complete address!

Peter Haynes who has been active in running (2:40 Seattle Marathon) and orienteering in the northwest for the past two years (when he wasn't busy delivering academic papers around the world related to his work in atmospheric science) has returned to England, his home and place of future employ. Peter can be reached via post at Queen's College, Cambridge CB3 9ET, ENGLAND.

Many thanks to Steve Smalley, Cindy Rinehart, Dick Foley, Dana Middleton and the rest of the staff and crew at KOMO TV for airing our O-spot.
1985 Western Canadian Orienteering Championships, August 31 & September 1

This year's meet was held in southeastern British Columbia about 15 miles north of Cranbrook. The terrain was mostly open and very runable. The foothills of the Canadian Rockies provided a magnificent backdrop for the competition area. Our Canadian hosts put on a superbly run event complete with clear weather, an outdoor barbecue, and dancing at the local tavern. The 138 competitors included the following Stateside orienteers:

A small but hardy group of orienteers turned out for the night meet at Discovery Park. Several inches of snow and temperatures in the low teens kept more reasonable people inside. The rest (less reasonable) of us enjoyed courses set by Mike Schuh and pizza and pumpkin pie at the potluck hosted by Mike after the meet. Thanks to Mike Schuh for setting courses, to Debbie Newell for registration, to Arnold Kay for timing, and to the Miller's for use of their lantern and blanket ... burrrrr!

The sunshine at the Snoqualmie Pass Ski-O was a pleasant relief from fog-bound Seattle. Thanks to Mike Schuh for single-handedly setting courses, registering, timing and tending the fire with wood supplied by his father, Tom. Thanks also to the Sons of Norway Trollhaugen Lodge caretaker, Don Ahlf, for helping groom the trails and provide logistical support for Mike. And thanks to all for helping to pickup controls, especially Don Denbo, Rick Breseman and Eileen Williams who designed their own freestyle course.

About 60 people attending with a dozen of those helping, made the Woodland park meet a success.
